    p.value = round(p.value, 4)
  ) %>%
  arrange(term, model) %>%
  print(n = Inf)

cat("\n── Model fit comparison ──\n")
cat("Base model      — McFadden R²:", round(mcfadden, 4),
    "| AUC:", round(auc(roc_obj), 4), "\n")
cat("Specialty model — McFadden R²:", round(mcfadden_sp, 4),
    "| AUC:", round(auc(roc_sp), 4), "\n")
```


```{r p3-logistic-specialty-plot}

or_comparison <- bind_rows(
  tidy(model_logistic, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) %>%
    mutate(model = "Base model"),
  tidy(model_logistic_specialty, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) %>%
    mutate(model = "Specialty-controlled")
) %>%
  filter(term %in% core_terms) %>%
  mutate(
    term = case_when(
      term == "log(Tot_Benes)"      ~ "log(Tot_Benes)",
      term == "log(srvcs_per_bene)" ~ "log(srvcs_per_bene)",
      term == "Bene_Avg_Risk_Scre"  ~ "Bene_Avg_Risk_Scre",
      term == "pct_dual"            ~ "pct_dual",
      term == "drug_providerTRUE"   ~ "drug_provider",
      term == "drug_provider"       ~ "drug_provider",
      term == "log(Tot_HCPCS_Cds)"  ~ "log(Tot_HCPCS_Cds)",
      TRUE ~ term
    )
  )

ggplot(or_comparison,
       aes(x = model, y = estimate,
           ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high,
           colour = model)) +
  geom_pointrange(size = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 1,
    linetype   = "dashed",
    colour     = "grey40",
    linewidth  = 0.6
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ term, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "Base model"           = "#2166AC",
      "Specialty-controlled" = "#C8102E"
    ),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Logistic Regression OR Comparison: Base vs Specialty-Controlled",
    subtitle = "Blue = base model; Red = specialty dummies added. Dashed line = OR of 1.",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Odds ratio"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, colour = "grey40"),
    strip.text    = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold")
  )

ggsave(
  here::here("docs", "figures", "specialty_controlled_or.png"),
  width = 9, height = 6, dpi = 300
)
```

::: pattern-box
**What the specialty robustness check reveals:**
All six core findings are stable or strengthen after controlling
for specialty group membership. No predictor attenuates toward
the null.

**\texttt{pct\_dual}}** moves from OR = 0.212 to OR = 0.168
after specialty adjustment --- strengthening rather than
attenuating. This directly rules out the most obvious
alternative explanation for the dual-eligibility finding:
high dual-eligibility providers are not simply concentrated
in inherently low-gap specialties. The association between
dual-eligibility burden and lower gap odds persists and
intensifies within specialty.

**\texttt{log(Tot\_Benes)}}** strengthens from 6.72 to 7.73,
indicating that within a given specialty, volume is an even
stronger differentiator of billing behavior than it appears
across specialties. Specialty and volume are partially
correlated --- high-volume providers tend to cluster in
certain specialties --- and controlling for specialty isolates
the within-specialty volume effect.

**\texttt{log(Tot\_HCPCS\_Cds)}}** attenuates modestly from
3.77 to 3.62, the only predictor that moves toward the null.
This is expected: practice breadth and specialty are related,
since different specialties have characteristically different
procedure repertoires. The attenuation is modest, confirming
that breadth captures something beyond specialty membership.

**\texttt{log(srvcs\_per\_bene)}}**, **\texttt{Bene\_Avg\_Risk\_Scre}}**,
and **\texttt{drug\_provider}}** are all stable across
specifications.

McFadden $R^2$ improves from 0.439 to 0.462 and AUC from
0.905 to 0.913 after adding specialty --- a modest improvement
confirming that specialty adds real but not decisive
explanatory power. The base model was not substantially
misspecified; specialty is a useful additional signal but
not a confound of the core findings.
:::
------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 4: Bootstrap & Permutation Test (RQ4)

::: fill-box
**Research Question 4:** Does the median standardized Medicare payment gap differ
significantly between Massachusetts providers whose patient panels have low versus
high proportions of dual-eligible beneficiaries?
:::

::: why-box
**Why Bootstrap & Permutation Tests?**

The payment gap distribution is heavily right-skewed with extreme outliers, making
parametric confidence intervals for the median unreliable. The median is not amenable
to a simple formula-based standard error under these distributional conditions, and
even after log transformation the tails remain heavy. Bootstrap resampling constructs
a confidence interval for each group's median empirically, making no distributional
assumptions. The permutation test then assesses whether the observed difference in
median payment gaps between the two groups could plausibly arise by chance under the
null hypothesis of no difference, without assuming normality in either group.

The dual-eligibility comparison is motivated directly by RQ3, which identified
`pct_dual` as a strong negative predictor of high-gap classification (OR = 0.186).
RQ4 examines whether this association persists unconditionally and quantifies its
magnitude in raw dollar terms.
:::

```{r p4-setup}
set.seed(6105)

ma_providers_rq4 <- ma_providers_rq3 %>%
  mutate(
    dual_group = ifelse(
      pct_dual > median(pct_dual),
      "High dual-eligibility",
      "Low dual-eligibility"
    )
  )

cat("── Group sizes ──\n")
table(ma_providers_rq4$dual_group)

low_gap  <- ma_providers_rq4$payment_gap[
  ma_providers_rq4$dual_group == "Low dual-eligibility"
]
high_gap <- ma_providers_rq4$payment_gap[
  ma_providers_rq4$dual_group == "High dual-eligibility"
]

obs_diff <- median(low_gap) - median(high_gap)

cat("\n── Observed medians ──\n")
cat("Low dual-eligibility:  $", scales::comma(round(median(low_gap))), "\n")
cat("High dual-eligibility: $", scales::comma(round(median(high_gap))), "\n")
cat("Observed difference:   $", scales::comma(round(obs_diff)), "\n")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 1: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for Each Group Median

::: why-box
**Why bootstrap the median separately for each group?**

The bootstrap constructs a confidence interval by repeatedly resampling each group
with replacement and computing the median on each resample. With 10,000 replicates,
the distribution of bootstrapped medians approximates the sampling distribution of
the observed median, from which percentile-based 95\% confidence intervals are
derived. No assumption is made about the shape of the payment gap distribution within
each group. Constructing separate CIs for each group's median makes the uncertainty
in each estimate visible before the formal hypothesis test.
:::

```{r p4-bootstrap}
B <- 10000

# Bootstrap CI for low dual-eligibility median
boot_low <- replicate(B, median(sample(low_gap, length(low_gap), replace = TRUE)))
ci_low   <- quantile(boot_low, c(0.025, 0.975))

# Bootstrap CI for high dual-eligibility median
boot_high <- replicate(B, median(sample(high_gap, length(high_gap), replace = TRUE)))
ci_high   <- quantile(boot_high, c(0.025, 0.975))

cat("── Bootstrap Results (B = 10,000) ──\n\n")

cat("Low dual-eligibility:\n")
cat("  Observed median: $", scales::comma(round(median(low_gap))), "\n")
cat("  95% CI:         [$", scales::comma(round(ci_low[1])),
    ", $", scales::comma(round(ci_low[2])), "]\n\n")

cat("High dual-eligibility:\n")
cat("  Observed median: $", scales::comma(round(median(high_gap))), "\n")
cat("  95% CI:         [$", scales::comma(round(ci_high[1])),
    ", $", scales::comma(round(ci_high[2])), "]\n")
```

```{r p4-bootstrap-plot, fig.cap="Bootstrap distributions of the median payment gap for low and high dual-eligibility providers (10,000 replicates each). Vertical solid lines mark the observed medians; dashed lines mark the 95% confidence interval bounds. Non-overlapping intervals indicate a statistically reliable difference."}
boot_df <- bind_rows(
  data.frame(group = "Low dual-eligibility",  median = boot_low),
  data.frame(group = "High dual-eligibility", median = boot_high)
)

ci_df <- data.frame(
  group  = c("Low dual-eligibility", "Low dual-eligibility",
             "High dual-eligibility", "High dual-eligibility"),
  x      = c(ci_low[1], ci_low[2], ci_high[1], ci_high[2]),
  ltype  = rep(c("lower", "upper"), 2)
)

obs_df <- data.frame(
  group = c("Low dual-eligibility", "High dual-eligibility"),
  x     = c(median(low_gap), median(high_gap))
)

ggplot(boot_df, aes(x = median, fill = group)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(data = obs_df, aes(xintercept = x),
             colour = "#C8102E", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(data = ci_df, aes(xintercept = x),
             colour = "#C8102E", linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Low dual-eligibility"  = "#C8102E",
    "High dual-eligibility" = "#2166AC"
  ), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  facet_wrap(~group, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(
    title    = "Bootstrap Distribution of Median Payment Gap by Dual-Eligibility Group",
    subtitle = "B = 10,000 replicates | Solid line = observed median | Dashed = 95% CI",
    x        = "Bootstrapped Median ($)",
    y        = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 13),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, colour = "grey40")
  )

ggsave(
  here::here("docs", "figures", "bootstrap_distribution.png"),
  width = 10, height = 5, dpi = 300
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 2: Permutation Test

::: why-box
**Why a permutation test?**

The permutation test asks: if group membership were assigned randomly, how often
would we observe a difference in median payment gaps as large as the one actually
observed? Under the null hypothesis of no difference, the group labels are
exchangeable. By randomly permuting the labels 10,000 times and computing the
difference in medians each time, we build an empirical null distribution against
which the observed difference is compared. The p-value is the proportion of permuted
differences that equal or exceed the observed difference in absolute magnitude.

**Hypotheses:**

$H_0$: The median payment gap is equal for low and high dual-eligibility providers.

$H_1$: The median payment gap differs between low and high dual-eligibility providers.
:::

```{r p4-permutation}
N_perm <- 10000

perm_diffs <- replicate(N_perm, {
  shuffled <- sample(ma_providers_rq4$dual_group)
  median(ma_providers_rq4$payment_gap[shuffled == "Low dual-eligibility"]) -
  median(ma_providers_rq4$payment_gap[shuffled == "High dual-eligibility"])
})

perm_p <- mean(abs(perm_diffs) >= abs(obs_diff))

cat("── Permutation Test Results (N = 10,000) ──\n")
cat("Observed difference: $", scales::comma(round(obs_diff)), "\n")
cat("Permutation p-value:", ifelse(perm_p == 0, "< 0.0001", round(perm_p, 4)), "\n")
```

```{r p4-permutation-plot, fig.cap="Empirical null distribution of 10,000 permuted differences in median payment gap. The red vertical lines mark the observed difference and its negative mirror. The proportion of permuted differences falling outside these lines gives the two-sided permutation p-value."}
ggplot(data.frame(diff = perm_diffs), aes(x = diff)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#2166AC", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept =  obs_diff, colour = "#C8102E", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -obs_diff, colour = "#C8102E", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(
    title    = "Permutation Null Distribution — Difference in Median Payment Gap",
    subtitle = paste0("N = 10,000 permutations | Observed difference = $",
                      scales::comma(round(obs_diff))),
    x        = "Permuted Difference in Medians ($)",
    y        = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 13),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, colour = "grey40")
  )

ggsave(
  here::here("docs", "figures", "permutation_distribution.png"),
  width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 3: Parametric Comparison

::: why-box
**Why compare to a parametric alternative?**

The Wilcoxon rank-sum test is a nonparametric alternative to the two-sample t-test
that does not assume normality but relies on asymptotic approximations for large
samples. Comparing its conclusion to the permutation test result assesses whether
the two approaches agree. The Hodges-Lehmann estimate reported by the Wilcoxon test
with `conf.int = TRUE` provides a pseudomedian difference that can be compared
directly to the bootstrap CIs.
:::

```{r p4-wilcoxon}
cat("── Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test ──\n")
wilcox.test(
  payment_gap ~ dual_group,
  data       = ma_providers_rq4,
  conf.int   = TRUE,
  conf.level = 0.95
)
```
